CSC 308 Final Project - Individual Portfolio

Emmanuella Obidike

April 2026

Project Overview

This project examines relationships between income, healthcare access, and health outcomes using county-level data. The analysis focuses on identifying patterns between economic factors and overall health rankings across selected states.

The dataset includes measures such as median household income, clinical care rankings, and health factors/outcomes indicators. The goal of the project is to understand how socioeconomic conditions influence healthcare quality and population health.

Individual Contribution – Emmanuella Obidike

Role: Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA), Statistical Analysis, and Interpretation

I was responsible for working with the cleaned 2021 dataset to explore relationships between income, clinical care, and health outcomes across selected states. My contribution focused on filtering the dataset, generating summary statistics, creating visualizations, and interpreting key patterns in the data.

Individual Contribution Summary

This portfolio represents the individual work of Emmanuella Obidike.

Primary responsibilities included:

  • Performing exploratory data analysis (EDA)
  • Generating summary statistics
  • Creating data visualizations using ggplot2
  • Conducting correlation analysis
  • Interpreting relationships between socioeconomic factors and healthcare access

All analysis and code in this document reflect my individual contributions to the overall team project.

Dataset and Preparation

I used the cleaned dataset Master_2021_Cleaned.csv, which contains county-level data including:

  • Median household income
  • Clinical care rankings
  • Health factors and health outcomes rankings
  • Demographic and health-related variables

To stay consistent with the project focus, I filtered the dataset to the following states:

  • Virginia
  • North Carolina
  • Maryland
  • Georgia
  • Florida
# Load dataset
data21 <- read.csv("processed_data/2021/Master_2021_Cleaned.csv")

# Filter selected states
states <- c("Virginia", "North Carolina", "Maryland", "Georgia", "Florida")

filtered_data <- data21 %>%
  filter(State %in% states)

Summary Statistics

summary_table <- filtered_data %>%
  group_by(State) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_income = mean(Median.Household.Income, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_clinical_rank = mean(Clinical_Care_Rank, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

summary_table
## # A tibble: 5 × 3
##   State          avg_income avg_clinical_rank
##   <chr>               <dbl>             <dbl>
## 1 Florida            55759.              29.4
## 2 Georgia            52195.              74.3
## 3 Maryland           80518.              12.5
## 4 North Carolina     52332.              49.4
## 5 Virginia           65552.              65.7

This provides a quick overview of differences between states.

Visualization 1: Income vs Clinical Care

ggplot(filtered_data, aes(x = Median.Household.Income, y = Clinical_Care_Rank)) +
  geom_point(color = "blue", alpha = 0.4) +
  labs(
    title = "Income vs Clinical Care (2021)",
    x = "Median Household Income",
    y = "Clinical Care Rank"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Interpretation

This scatter plot shows a general downward trend.
As income increases, clinical care rank tends to improve (lower rank = better care).

Visualization 2: Average Income by State

ggplot(summary_table, aes(x = State, y = avg_income)) +
  geom_col(fill = "purple") +
  labs(
    title = "Average Income by State (2021)",
    x = "State",
    y = "Average Income"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Interpretation

Maryland has the highest average income, while Georgia and North Carolina are lower.

Correlation: Income vs Clinical Care

correlation <- cor(
  filtered_data$Median.Household.Income,
  filtered_data$Clinical_Care_Rank,
  use = "complete.obs"
)

correlation
## [1] -0.4827049

Interpretation

The correlation is about -0.48, showing a moderate negative relationship.

Visualization 3: Health Factors vs Health Outcomes

ggplot(filtered_data, aes(x = Health_Factors_Rank, y = Health_Outcomes_Rank)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, color = "darkgreen") +
  labs(
    title = "Health Factors vs Health Outcomes",
    x = "Health Factors Rank",
    y = "Health Outcomes Rank"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Interpretation

This suggests that counties with poorer health factors tend to have worse health outcomes, indicating a strong relationship between environmental conditions and overall population health.

Correlation: Health Factors vs Outcomes

correlation2 <- cor(
  filtered_data$Health_Factors_Rank,
  filtered_data$Health_Outcomes_Rank,
  use = "complete.obs"
)

correlation2
## [1] 0.9185608

Interpretation

The correlation is about 0.91, which is very strong.

Conclusion

The analysis reveals clear relationships between socioeconomic factors and healthcare access.

Higher income levels are generally associated with better clinical care rankings, suggesting that economic stability plays a key role in access to healthcare resources. Also, strong relationships between health factors and health outcomes show that environmental and behavioral conditions significantly influence overall population health.

Differences observed across states highlight how geographic and demographic variations contribute to disparities in healthcare access. These findings emphasize the importance of addressing socioeconomic inequalities in order to improve healthcare accessibility and outcomes.

Overall, this project demonstrates how data visualization and exploratory analysis can transform complex healthcare data into insights that are more accessible and meaningful for a broader audience.

Individual Contribution (Emmanuella Obidike)

  • Created summary tables
  • Built visualizations
